summary: Researched HyMem (arxiv:2602.13933) and the dual-layer episodic/semantic memory architecture pattern for LLM agents. Surveyed 7 papers (HyMem, HiMem, Synapse, SimpleMem, A-MEM, Mem0, MemGPT), extracted benchmark results, architecture details, and practical LanceDB implementation patterns. Saved findings to persistent memory.
findings: HyMem (arxiv:2602.13933, Feb 2026) is a real paper: dual-granularity L1 (summaries) + L2 (raw text) with dynamic routing. 89.55% LOCOMO overall, 92.6% token reduction vs full-context.
HyMem's biggest win is multi-hop: 88.16% vs 46.89% full-context (+41.3 pp) — this is the closest evidence to the '~40% improvement' claim.
HyMem uses summary/raw granularity layers, NOT episodic/semantic. It lacks write-time consolidation and cross-session knowledge evolution.
Episodic memory (what happened): append-only, cheap, deterministic window retrieval. Semantic memory (what it means): LLM-distilled, async write, hybrid vector+BM25 retrieval.
HiMem (arxiv:2601.06377) achieves 80.71% LOCOMO GPT-Score vs A-MEM 51.88% using immutable episodes + evolving Note Memory with ADD/UPDATE/DELETE reconsolidation.
SimpleMem ablation: removing consolidation drops multi-hop accuracy by 31.3% — consolidation is the key differentiator for dual-layer over flat storage.

For LanceDB: two tables (episodes + semantic), parallel retrieval (deterministic window scan + hybrid search), async background consolidation at τ=0.85 affinity threshold, freshest episodic wins on conflict.
